coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterization of photocatalytic carbon dots-sensitized electrospun titania nanostructured fibers
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Haopeng
Zhu, Yihua
Cao, Huimin
Yang, Xiaoling
Li, Chunzhong
coredata.dc:description: The carbon dots (CDs) are new functional carbon-aceous materials. Compared to conventional dye molecules and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs are superior in chemical inertness and low toxicity. The TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers were fabricated by combining the electrospinning technique and reflux method. Compared with the pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers and P25, the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers exhibited enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of photodegradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers could be attributed to the presence of CDs embedded in TiO2 nanostructured fibers. The CD can work as a photosensitizer in the degradation. Furthermore, the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers could be easily handled and recycled due to their one-dimensional nanostructural property.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Inorganic compounds
A. Nanostructures
A. Semiconductors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic activity of TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers; (b and c) HRTEM image of the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers.
FTIR spectra of TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers, amine-TiO2 nanostructured fibers and pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers.
XRD patterns of the P25, pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers and the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers.

Diffuse reflectance UV–visible spectra of the P25, pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers and the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers. (For interpretation of the references to color in the text legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)

The inverted fluorescence microscope of (a) TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers under bright field; (b) TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers under violet light; (c) pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers under bright field; and (d) pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers under violet ray.
The photocatalytic decolorization of RhB under visible light irradiation.
Postulate mechanism of the visible light-induced photodegradation of RhB with the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers.
Photocatalytic activity of TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers for RhB degradation with three times of cycling uses.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterization of photocatalytic carbon dots-sensitized electrospun titania nanostructured fibers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: ► The TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers are fabricated by using APS combining the electrospinning TiO2 nanostructured fibers and CDs. ► The CD can work as a photosensitizer in the degradation of rhodamine B under visible light irradiation. ► The TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers exhibit enhanced photocatalytic efficiency and can be easily handled and recycled.

The carbon dots (CDs) are new functional carbon-aceous materials. Compared to conventional dye molecules and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs are superior in chemical inertness and low toxicity. The TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers were fabricated by combining the electrospinning technique and reflux method. Compared with the pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers and P25, the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers exhibited enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of photodegradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers could be attributed to the presence of CDs embedded in TiO2 nanostructured fibers. The CD can work as a photosensitizer in the degradation. Furthermore, the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers could be easily handled and recycled due to their one-dimensional nanostructural property.

ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalyst which can degrade organic pollutants has become an active area of research because of environmental problems such as air and water pollution produced by some industries [1]. Titanium dioxide has been widely used as a photocatalyst due to its excellent chemical stability, low toxicity, low-cost, and high photocatalytic activity [2–5]. Nonetheless, TiO2 has a wide bandgap (3.2eV), it can only be excited with ultra violet (UV) light, which is less than 5% of solar light [6]. This problem limited the applications of pure TiO2.

In order to extend the photoresponse of TiO2 to the visible region, there were many methods in the past researches: (1) doping with transition metal ions (e.g. Co2+, Fe3+) [7,8] or non-metals (e.g. N, C) [9,10]; (2) heterostructuring of titania by codoping (e.g. Ce–I–TiO2) [11] and mixed-phase titania (e.g. anatase-brookite) [12,13]; (3) sensitization with organic dyes and organic–inorganic hybrid dyes (e.g. N3, copper (II) phthalocyanine) [14,15], narrow band-gap semiconductors (e.g. CdS) [16], aurivillius oxides (e.g. Bi2WO6) [17], noble metals (e.g. Au, Ag) [18,19] or functional carbon-aceous materials (e.g. graphene, C60, carbon particles) [20–23]. And the electrons ejected by UV illumination to the conduction band (CB) can reduce dissolved oxygen (ORR) on the carbon surface to produce hydrogen peroxide, which is good electron acceptor.

The carbon dots (CDs) are new functional carbon-aceous materials. Generally, CDs are nanocrystals (diameter <10nm) composed of graphitic carbon [24]. As a carbon-aceous material, it has wide visible light absorption at the wavelength of 400–800nm [25]. As a new type of quantum dots (QDs), CDs has a potential to replace traditional QDs in both health and environmental applications [26]. Similar to the traditional QDs [27], it can tune light absorption to cover a large fraction of the solar spectrum simply by changing their sizes, and the large QDs extinction coefficients and the multiple exciton generation phenomenon can facilitate the interface reaction of photocatalysis [28,29]. Compared to conventional dye molecules and semiconductor quantum dots, such as CdSe, CDs are superior in chemical inertness, low toxicity [30]. They are also shown to be both excellent electron donators and acceptors [31]. It is also environmentally friendly, low cost and have extensive source [32,33].

The photocatalyst for practical applications should have superior catalytic activity and be easily synthesized, fixed, and reclaimed [34]. Compared with conventional bulk material, nanostructured TiO2 exhibits superior photocatalytic activity because of its high surface area and strong organic adsorption capacity [35]. However, down sizing of materials results in a number of negative problems, for instance, they become easy to aggregate and hard to reutilize [36]. Electrospinning, as a well-known easy, effective and convenient method which can generate nanostructured fibers with diameters ranging from nanometer to micrometer, has been employed in many applications [37–41]. The electrospinning TiO2 nanostructured fibers exhibit significant advantages, they have large surface areas and light harvesting ability, exhibit superior catalytic properties, they can provide a large number of surface-active sites to be accessible for reactants more easily and effectively, are easily recycled, and as fibers, the TiO2 can be fixed and reclaimed easily [42–47].

In this work, we use the CDs to sensitize the TiO2 nanostructured fibers to synthetize a new photocatalyst. We combined the electrospinning technique (for TiO2 nanostructured fibers) and reflux approach (for connecting CDs) to fabricate TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers. These TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers could be easily recycled without decrease of the photocatalytic activity due to their one-dimensional nanostructure property, and enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible-light irradiation owing to the sensitization of CDs.

Tetrabutyl titanate (Ti(OBu)4), citric acid were supplied by Shanghai Linfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. EtOH, rhodamine B (RhB) and acetic acid were supplied by Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP, MW=1,300,000) was provided by Alfa Aesar. 3-Aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APS) was provided by J&K Scientific Ltd. P25 was purchased from Degussa. All the chemicals were used without further purification.

1.0g Ti(OBu)4 was mixed with 2mL acetic acid and 2mL ethanol in a beaker, stirred for 20min, then added 4mL ethanol and 0.4g PVP, magnetic stirred for 2h, after that, the TiO2-PVP nanostructured fibers were electrospun from the solution under the conditions of voltage of 12kV, interelectrode distance of 15cm. The TiO2-PVP nanostructured fibers were calcined at 500°C to crystallize TiO2 and remove the PVP template.

50mg TiO2 nanostructured fibers grinded in mortar, added 50mL ultra-pure water, dispersed by ultrasonic, then added 0.5mL APS (the use of APS is connecting the TiO2 nanostructured fibers and CDs), refluxed at 100°C for 24h, after centrifugated at 8000rpm for 5min, the nanostructured fibers were washed with ultra-pure water for three times, and added 5mL of CDs aqueous solution (the CDs were prepared with mesoporous silica spheres as nanoreactors by using an impregnation method [48]), stirred for 6h at 50°C and centrifugated at 8000rpm, the precipitate washed with ultra-pure water until the supernatant could not see fluorescence at 365nm UV lamp to obtain TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers. At last, the fibers disperse in ultra-pure water to preservation.

The photocatalytic activities of the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers, pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers and P25 were evaluated by photodegradation of RhB. A 150W high-pressure Xenon lamp with a cut-off glass filter transmitting λ >400nm placed 10cm away from the reaction vessel was used as the light source. The illumination intensity was about 50mWcm−2. The selected photocatalyst (30mg) was dispersed in 50mL RhB aqueous solution (10−5 M). After stirred in the dark for 60min, the solution obtained a good dispersion and reached adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the organic molecules and the catalyst surface. At given intervals of illumination, the samples (3mL) of the reaction solution were taken out and centrifuged. Finally, the decrease in the concentration of dyes in filtrates were analyzed by a UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer at λ =553nm.

The morphologies of nanostructured fibers were observed by the scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-6360LV). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) measurements were performed on TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers samples which were drop-cast onto a carbon-coated Cu grid. Wide-angle (10–80°, 40kV/100mA) X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were recorded on a Rigaku D/max 2550 VB/PC diffractometer using nickel-filtered Cu Kα radiation with the wavelength of λ =1.5406Å. The inverted fluorescence microscope images of TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers were obtained using a inverted microscope NIKON TE2000-U. FTIR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 5700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. The diffuse reflectance UV–visible spectra were obtained using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Varian, Cary 500). The degradation of RhB in water was measured by a Unico UV-2102PC spectrophotometer.

Fig. 1(a) shows the typical scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers for representation. It demonstrates that the length of these randomly oriented nanofibers could reach several micrometers, and the diameters of those nanostructured fibers ranged from 250 to 500nm. The vast majority of nanostructured fibers are uniform. In order to study the microstructure of the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) observations were carried out. Fig. 1(b) shows that the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers have a porous structure. Fig. 1(c) shows some lattice fringes of the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers, some lattice observed correspond to the interplanar distance of 0.345nm, which most likely to be (101) lattice plane of the anatase TiO2 (PDF No. 21-1272). Besides, a hexagonal lattice are observed in Fig. 1(c), the interplanar spacing of 0.213nm correspond to the (100) plane of graphite carbon (PDF No. 41-1487).

The functional groups present in the modified nanostructured fibers can be studied using FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra of the pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers, amine functionalized TiO2 using APS and CDs-TiO2 nanostructured fibers. The FTIR spectrum of the amine-TiO2 nanostructured fibers shows the presence of a new peak 1270cm−1, which is attributed to CN stretching mode. And the CDs-TiO2 nanostructured fibers shows the presence of new peaks and it suggests that the CDs have chemically immobilized on the surfaces of the nanostructured fibers. The peak around 650cm−1 is representative of TiO2 matrices. The peaks around 1633, 1715 and 1230cm−1 correspond to the presence of CC, CO and CO stretching vibration in the CDs, respectively. The peak around 1400cm−1 correspond to CH in-plane bending vibration in the CDs or APS, and the peak around 3430cm−1 correspond to the OH stretching vibration in the CDs.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the P25, pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers and TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers are shown in Fig. 3 . The curves in Fig. 3 reveals that the crystal phase of TiO2 nanostructured fibers is anatase with the diffraction peaks at about 2θ =25.38, 37.90, 48.16, 53.98, and 55.18, 62.68, 68.88, 70.44, 75.22 which can be perfectly indexed to the 101, 004, 200, 105, and 211, 204, 116, 220, 215 crystal faces of anatase TiO2 respectively (PDF No. 21-1272). The peak at 2θ =27.44 corresponds to the 110 crystal faces of rutile structure, which suggests all three TiO2 contain a small amount of rutile.

The diffuse reflectance UV–visible spectra of the pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers, TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers and P25 are shown in Fig. 4 . As observed in Fig. 4, the diffuse reflectance spectra of the TiO2 nanostructured fibers and P25 only exhibit the fundamental absorption band in the UV region, and there are no more absorption in visible wavelengths. Notably, the curve of TiO2-CDs shows significant enhancement of light absorption at the wavelength of 400–800nm. Moreover, the curve has a clear red shift, which confirms doping of TiO2 with N and/or C in the composite fibers as reported by Yin et al. and Sato [49,50]. The reason for the absorption of visible light can be attributed two parts: one was from the CDs itself; the other is a joint electronic system formed between TiO2 and CDs, which give rise in synergistic properties. The above results indicate that the sensitization of TiO2 with CDs can extend the absorbance spectrum of TiO2 into visible region.

The hybrid structure of the resulting composite of TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers was characterized by inverted fluorescence microscope. The result shows that the light image (Fig. 5(a)) and the fluorescent image (Fig. 5(b)) match well. The images reveal that the CDs exhibit strong fluorescence and a good dispersion. As a comparison, the light image (Fig. 5(c)) and fluorescent image (Fig. 5(d)) of pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers are taken. The result shows that the fluorescence intensity of pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers is very weak, the fluorescent of TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers is derived from CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic decolorization of RhB dye molecules was used to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers under visible light irradiation. P25, TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers and pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers were chosen as the reference photocatalysts for comparison. The photocatalytic test was carried out after stirring in the dark for 60min, the solution obtained a good dispersion and reached adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the organic molecules and the catalyst surface. The photocatalytic results are shown in Fig. 6 , which reveals that the RhB molecules are almost not degradable without photocatalyst under visible light irradiation, and are degradable with P25, CDs-TiO2 nanostructured fibers and pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers. Both pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers and P25 are the nanostructure materials, their large surface areas are conducive to the photocatalytic decolorization of RhB, which is in accordance with literature [51]. All of the catalyst contain a small amount of rutile, the mixed-phase TiO2 materials have unique charge transfer and recombination dynamics, fast diffusion of charge carriers to the surface, or an interface that enhances the interfacial charge transfer processes [52]. While the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers further improve the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation due to their wider region light-response than pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers or P25 and the coupling of CDs and TiO2 can reduce the recombination of the photoinduced electrons and holes. The postulate mechanism of the visible light-induced photodegradation of RhB with the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers is shown in Fig. 7 . In an aqueous phase, under visible light irradiation, on the surface of sensitizers CDs formate holes (h+) and electrons (e−).
(1) h+ +OH− → OH
(2)
OH+RhB→CO2 +H2O

The excited electron is then injected into the conduction band of TiO2 to initiate a reaction. Dissolved oxygen molecules (O2) react with conduction band electrons (e−) to yield superoxide radical anions (O2 −), which on protonation generated the hydroperoxy radicals (HO2 ), producing hydroxyl radicals (OH), the quantity of OH increased and the efficiency of photocatalysis improved [53].
(3) e− +O2 →O2 −
(4) O2 − +H2O→HO2
+OH−
(5) HO2
+H2O→H2O2 + OH
(6) H2O2 →2OH

In general, the absorption of a photocatalyst should mirror the action of a photocatalytic reaction [54]. From the diffuse reflectance UV–visible spectra in Fig. 4, the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers show more enhanced visible light absorption intensity than that of P25, the increase in the absorption magnitude indicate an increase in the number of electron–hole pairs formed on the photocatalyst surface [55]. Therefore, it is reasonable to observe the higher photocatalytic activity over TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers than P25 toward the degradation of RhB under the irradiation of visible light. The CDs have some very weak up-converted PL properties, up-converted PL properties can enhance the photocatalytic activity of photocatalyst [56], but the up-converted PL properties of our CDs is too weak, the influence of this aspect can be ignored.

Fig. 8 shows the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers under visible light irradiation with three time cycling uses. It shows that the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers can be easily recovered by centrifugation or sedimentation, and will greatly promote their industrial application to eliminate the organic pollutants from waste water.

In summary, we describe herein an easy and low-cost method to synthesize TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers combined with electrospinning technique. The core/shell nanostructured fibers have remarkable light absorption in the visible region. The investigation of photocatalytic ability indicated that the TiO2-CDs exhibited more enhanced photocatalytic activity in the decomposition of RhB under visible-light irradiation compared with the pure TiO2 nanostructured fibers and P25, Furthermore, these TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers could be easily recycled without decrease of the photocatalytic activity due to their one-dimensional nanostructure property. They are expected that the TiO2-CDs nanostructured fibers will greatly promote their industrial application to eliminate the organic pollutants from waste water.
